From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Ordinary (officer)](/w/index.php?title=Ordinary_(officer)&redirect=no "Ordinary (officer)"))

Ecclesiastical title for local authorities

"Hierarch" redirects here. For the characters in the video game series *Halo*, see [Hierarchs (*Halo*)](/wiki/Hierarchs_(Halo) "Hierarchs (Halo)"). For other uses, see [Hierarchy (disambiguation)](/wiki/Hierarchy_(disambiguation) "Hierarchy (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Alencastre_Window.jpg/250px-Alencastre_Window.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alencastre_Window.jpg)

[Pope Pius XI](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XI "Pope Pius XI") (left), depicted in this window at the [Cathedral of Our Lady of Peace](/wiki/Cathedral_of_Our_Lady_of_Peace "Cathedral of Our Lady of Peace"), [Honolulu](/wiki/Honolulu "Honolulu"), was ordinary of the universal Church as well as the [Diocese of Rome](/wiki/Diocese_of_Rome "Diocese of Rome") from 1922 to 1939. Bishop [Stephen Alencastre](/wiki/Stephen_Alencastre "Stephen Alencastre") (right), [Apostolic Vicar](/wiki/Vicar_apostolic "Vicar apostolic") of the [Sandwich Islands](/wiki/Hawaiian_Islands "Hawaiian Islands"), was the ordinary of what is now the [Diocese of Honolulu](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Honolulu "Roman Catholic Diocese of Honolulu").

An **ordinary** (from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *ordinarius*) is an officer of a church or civic authority who by reason of office has **ordinary power** to execute laws.

Such officers are found in hierarchically organised churches of [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity") which have an [ecclesiastical legal system](/wiki/Canon_law "Canon law").[[1]](#cite_note-rc-ord-1) For example, diocesan bishops are ordinaries in the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church")[[1]](#cite_note-rc-ord-1) and the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England").[[2]](#cite_note-2) In [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity"), a corresponding officer is called a **hierarch**[[3]](#cite_note-3) (from [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") ἱεράρχης *hierarkhēs* "president of sacred rites, high-priest"[[4]](#cite_note-4) which comes in turn from τὰ ἱερά *ta hiera*, "the sacred rites" and ἄρχω *arkhō*, "I rule").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

During at least the 17th and 18th centuries the title of Ordinary was used for prison chaplains in England, such as the one who attended condemned men before their execution at [Newgate](/wiki/Ordinary_of_Newgate%27s_Account "Ordinary of Newgate's Account").

## Ordinary power

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ordinary_(church_officer)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Ordinary power")]

In [canon law](/wiki/Canon_law "Canon law"), the power to govern the church is divided into the power to make laws (legislative), enforce the laws (executive), and to judge based on the law (judicial).[[6]](#cite_note-6) An official exercises power to govern either because he holds an office to which the law grants governing power or because someone with governing power has delegated it to him. Ordinary power is the former, while the latter is delegated power.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The office with ordinary power could possess the governing power itself (proper ordinary power) or instead it could have the ordinary power of agency, the inherent power to exercise someone else's power ([vicarious](/wiki/Vicar "Vicar") ordinary power).[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The law vesting ordinary power could either be ecclesiastical law, i.e. the positive enactments that the church has established for itself, or divine law, i.e. the laws which were given to the Church by God.[[9]](#cite_note-9) As an example of divinely instituted ordinaries, when [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") established the Church, he also established the [episcopate](/wiki/Episcopate "Episcopate") and the [primacy of Peter](/wiki/Primacy_of_Peter "Primacy of Peter"), endowing the offices with power to govern the Church.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Thus, in the Catholic Church, the office of successor of Simon Peter and the office of diocesan bishop possess their ordinary power even in the absence of positive enactments from the Church.

Many officers possess ordinary power but, due to their lack of ordinary executive power, are not called ordinaries. The best example of this phenomenon is the office of [judicial vicar](/wiki/Judicial_vicar "Judicial vicar"), a.k.a. *[officialis](/wiki/Officialis "Officialis")*. The judicial vicar only has authority through his office to exercise the diocesan bishop's power to judge cases.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Though the vicar has vicarious ordinary judicial power, he is not an ordinary because he lacks ordinary executive power. A [vicar general](/wiki/Vicar_general "Vicar general"), however, has authority through his office to exercise the diocesan bishop's executive power.[[12]](#cite_note-12) He is therefore an ordinary because of this vicarious ordinary executive power.

## Catholic Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ordinary_(church_officer)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Catholic Church")]

See also: [Hierarchy of the Catholic Church § Ordinaries and local ordinaries](/wiki/Hierarchy_of_the_Catholic_Church#Ordinaries_and_local_ordinaries "Hierarchy of the Catholic Church"), and [Bishops in the Catholic Church](/wiki/Bishops_in_the_Catholic_Church "Bishops in the Catholic Church")

### Local ordinaries and hierarchs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ordinary_(church_officer)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Local ordinaries and hierarchs")]

Local ordinaries exercise ordinary power and are ordinaries in [particular churches](/wiki/Particular_church "Particular church").[[13]](#cite_note-13) The following [clerics](/wiki/Cleric "Cleric") are local ordinaries:

* The [Bishop of Rome](/wiki/Bishop_of_Rome "Bishop of Rome") (the pope) is ordinary for the whole [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* In [Eastern Catholic churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches"), [patriarchs](/wiki/Patriarch "Patriarch"), [major archbishops](/wiki/Major_archbishop "Major archbishop"), and [metropolitans](/wiki/Metropolitan_bishop "Metropolitan bishop") have ordinary power of governance for the whole territory of their respective autonomous particular churches.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* [Diocesan](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese")/[eparchial](/wiki/Eparchy "Eparchy") [bishops](/wiki/Bishop_(Catholic_Church)#Diocesan_Bishops_or_Eparchs "Bishop (Catholic Church)")/[eparchs](/wiki/Eparchy "Eparchy")
* Other [prelates](/wiki/Prelate "Prelate") who head, even if only temporarily, a [particular church](/wiki/Particular_church "Particular church") or a community equivalent to it. Canon 368 of the *Code of Canon Law* lists five Latin jurisdictional areas that are considered equivalent to a diocese.[[17]](#cite_note-17) These are headed by:
  + A [territorial prelate](/wiki/Territorial_prelature "Territorial prelature"), formerly called a prelate *nullius dioceseos* (of no diocese), in charge of a geographical area that has not yet been raised to the level of diocese
  + A [territorial abbot](/wiki/Territorial_abbey "Territorial abbey"), in charge of an area, which in mission countries can be quite vast, associated with an abbey
  + An [apostolic vicar](/wiki/Apostolic_vicariate "Apostolic vicariate") (normally a bishop of a [titular see](/wiki/Titular_see "Titular see")), in charge of an apostolic vicariate, usually in a mission country, not yet ready to be made a diocese
  + An [apostolic prefect](/wiki/Apostolic_prefecture "Apostolic prefecture") (usually not a bishop), in charge of an apostolic prefecture, not yet ready to be made an apostolic vicariate
  + A [permanent apostolic administrator](/wiki/Apostolic_administrator "Apostolic administrator"), in charge of a geographical area that for serious reasons cannot be made a diocese.
* To these may be added:
  + An [apostolic exarch](/wiki/Exarch#Modern_Eastern_Catholic_churches "Exarch") (normally a bishop of a titular see), in charge of an apostolic exarchate—not yet ready to be made an eparchy—for the faithful of an Eastern Catholic Church in an area that is situated outside the home territory of that Eastern Church
  + A [military ordinariate](/wiki/Military_ordinariate "Military ordinariate")
  + A [personal prelate](/wiki/Personal_Prelate "Personal Prelate"), in charge of a group of persons without regard to geography: the only personal prelature existing is that of [Opus Dei](/wiki/Opus_Dei "Opus Dei")
  + An [apostolic administrator](/wiki/Apostolic_administrator "Apostolic administrator") of a personal apostolic administration: only one exists, the [Personal Apostolic Administration of Saint John Mary Vianney](/wiki/Personal_Apostolic_Administration_of_Saint_John_Mary_Vianney "Personal Apostolic Administration of Saint John Mary Vianney")
  + An ordinary of a [personal ordinariate](/wiki/Personal_ordinariate "Personal ordinariate") for former Anglicans[[18]](#cite_note-18)
  + A superior of an [autonomous mission](/wiki/Mission_sui_iuris "Mission sui iuris")
* Of somewhat similar standing is the [diocesan administrator](/wiki/Diocesan_administrator "Diocesan administrator") (formerly called a *vicar capitular*) elected to govern a diocese during a vacancy. Apart from certain limitations of nature and law, he has, on a caretaker basis, the same obligations and powers as a diocesan bishop.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Occasionally an apostolic administrator is appointed by the Holy See to run a vacant diocese, or even a diocese whose bishop is incapacitated or otherwise impeded.

Also classified as local ordinaries, although they do not head a particular church or equivalent community are:

* [Vicars general](/wiki/Vicar_general "Vicar general") and [protosyncelli](/wiki/Protosyncellus "Protosyncellus")
* [Episcopal vicars](/wiki/Vicar_general "Vicar general") and [syncelli](/wiki/Protosyncellus "Protosyncellus")

### Ordinaries who are not local ordinaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ordinary_(church_officer)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ordinaries who are not local ordinaries")]

Major superiors of [religious institutes](/wiki/Religious_institute "Religious institute") (including [abbots](/wiki/Abbot "Abbot")) and of [societies of apostolic life](/wiki/Society_of_apostolic_life "Society of apostolic life") are ordinaries of their respective memberships, but not local ordinaries.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Eastern Orthodox Churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ordinary_(church_officer)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Eastern Orthodox Churches")]

In the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), a hierarch (ruling bishop) holds uncontested authority within the boundaries of his own diocese; no other bishop may perform any [sacerdotal](/wiki/Sacerdotal "Sacerdotal") functions without the ruling bishop's express invitation. The violation of this rule is called *eispēdēsis* ([Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): εἰσπήδησις, "trespassing", literally "jumping in"), and is uncanonical. Ultimately, all bishops in the Church are equal, regardless of any title they may enjoy ([Patriarch](/wiki/Patriarch "Patriarch"), [Metropolitan](/wiki/Metropolitan_bishop "Metropolitan bishop"), [Archbishop](/wiki/Archbishop "Archbishop"), etc.). The role of the bishop in the Orthodox Church is both hierarchical and sacramental.[[21]](#cite_note-Ware_21-21)

This pattern of governance dates back to the earliest centuries of Christianity, as witnessed by the writings of [Ignatius of Antioch](/wiki/Ignatius_of_Antioch "Ignatius of Antioch") (c. 100 AD):

> The bishop in each Church presides in the place of God.... Let no one do any of the things which concern the Church without the bishop.... Wherever the bishop appears, there let the people be, just as wherever Jesus Christ is, there is the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic").

And it is the bishop's primary and distinctive task to celebrate the [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist"), "the medicine of immortality."[[21]](#cite_note-Ware_21-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)
Saint [Cyprian of Carthage](/wiki/Cyprian_of_Carthage "Cyprian of Carthage") (258 AD) wrote:

> The episcopate is a single whole, in which each bishop enjoys full possession. So is the Church a single whole, though it spreads far and wide into a multitude of churches and its fertility increases.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

[Bishop Kallistos (Ware)](/wiki/Timothy_Ware "Timothy Ware") wrote:

> There are many churches, but only One Church; many *episcopi* but only one episcopate."[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In [Eastern Orthodox Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy"), the church is not seen as a monolithic, centralized institution, but rather as existing in its fullness in each local body. The church is defined Eucharistically:

> in each particular community gathered around its bishop; and at every local celebration of the Eucharist it is the *whole* Christ who is present, not just a part of Him. Therefore, each local community, as it celebrates the Eucharist ... is the church in its fullness."[[21]](#cite_note-Ware_21-21)

An Eastern Orthodox bishop's authority comes from his election and [consecration](/wiki/Consecration "Consecration"). He is, however, subject to the [Sacred Canons](/wiki/Canon_Law "Canon Law") of the Eastern Orthodox Church, and answers to the [Synod of Bishops](/wiki/Synod_of_Bishops_(Orthodox) "Synod of Bishops (Orthodox)") to which he belongs. In case an Orthodox bishop is overruled by his local synod, he retains the right of [appeal](/wiki/Appeal "Appeal") ([Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): Ἔκκλητον, *Ékklēton*) to his ecclesiastical superior (e.g. a Patriarch) and his synod.
